metadata.title: The Average of a Continuous Function Is a Slope

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Daylight hours follow a sine wave through the year. What is the average number of daylight hours over an entire summer? The answer is an integral — and it turns out to equal the average slope of the antiderivative. Average value is rise over run.

card.copy: The Average of a Continuous Function Is a Slope

beats.narration_text: For a discrete set of values, the average is the sum divided by the count. Extending this to a continuous function requires replacing the sum with an integral and the count with the interval length.

production_viz.label: N sample bars under sine curve becoming continuous integral — discrete sum transitioning to integral

beats.narration_text: How do you define the average value of a continuously varying function over an interval — and why does that definition lead directly to integration?

card.copy: Average of a list: sum/count. Average of a continuous function over an interval: how do you define it without a finite list?

beats.narration_text: Sample the function at N evenly spaced points over [a, b]. Spacing: dx equals (b minus a) over N. Average equals sum of f(x_i) divided by N. Substitute N equals (b minus a) over dx.

production_viz.label: N sample points on [a,b] with spacing dx — average = Σf(xᵢ)/N, substituting N = (b-a)/dx

beats.narration_text: The sum becomes 1 over (b minus a) times sum of f(x_i) times dx. As N goes to infinity, the sum becomes the integral of f from a to b. Average value equals 1 over (b minus a) times the integral of f.

production_viz.label: Derivation steps: (1/(b-a))Σf(xᵢ)dx → as N→∞ → (1/(b-a))∫f dx — integral emerges from sampling

beats.narration_text: For the sine function from 0 to π: average equals 1 over π times the integral of sin(x) from 0 to π. The antiderivative of sin(x) is negative cosine. Evaluated from 0 to π: negative cosine π minus negative cosine 0 equals 1 plus 1 equals 2. Average equals 2 over π, about 0.637.
The connection to slopes: average value equals F(b) minus F(a) over (b minus a), where F is the antiderivative. This is exactly the average slope of F over [a, b] — rise over run.

production_viz.label: Antiderivative -cos(x) with rise/run = 2/π marked — average value = average slope of antiderivative

beats.narration_text: Intuition check: the sine curve spends more time near its peak value of 1 than near 0, where it passes through quickly. So the average should be above 0.5. It is: 2 over π is about 0.637. The formula captures this asymmetry.

production_viz.label: Sine curve spending more time near peak than near 0 — average line at 2/π ≈ 0.637 above 0.5

beats.narration_text: This formula appears throughout physics and engineering: average power from alternating current, mean speed of a gas molecule, average position of a quantum particle — all use the same integral average.

production_viz.label: Applications of integral average: AC power, gas molecule speed, quantum particle position — same formula

beats.narration_text: Average value of f over [a,b] equals 1 over (b minus a) times the integral of f, which equals F(b) minus F(a) over (b minus a) — the average slope of the antiderivative. Sine from 0 to π: 2 over π approximately 0.637. Average value equals rise over run.

card.copy: Average value = (1/(b-a))∫f dx = average slope of antiderivative. sin(x) on [0,π]: 2/π ≈ 0.637 — above 0.5 because sine spends more time near its peak.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
